@@ -117,7 +117,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","title":"[ubu.1] Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh library","description":"# Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh Library ## Purpose Core library implementing Ubuntu upgrade detection and execution logic. ## File Location `scripts/lib/ubuntu_upgrade.sh` ## Functions to Implement ### Version Detection ```bash # Get current Ubuntu version as comparable number (e.g., 2404 for 24.04) ubuntu_get_version_number() # Get current Ubuntu version string (e.g., "24.04") ubuntu_get_version_string() # Compare two version numbers (returns 0 if $1 >= $2) ubuntu_version_gte() { local v1="$1" v2="$2"; } ``` ### Upgrade Path Calculation ```bash # Get next available upgrade version (queries do-release-upgrade -c) ubuntu_get_next_upgrade() # Calculate full upgrade path from current to target # Returns newline-separated list of versions ubuntu_calculate_upgrade_path() { local target="$1"; } # Get the latest available Ubuntu version ubuntu_get_latest_available() ``` ### Pre-upgrade Checks ```bash # Run all pre-upgrade validations # Returns 0 if all checks pass, 1 with error details if not ubuntu_preflight_checks() # Individual checks (called by ubuntu_preflight_checks) ubuntu_check_disk_space() # Need ~5GB free ubuntu_check_network() # Test connectivity ubuntu_check_apt_state() # No broken packages ubuntu_check_not_docker() # Upgrades don't work in containers ``` ### Upgrade Execution ```bash # Perform single-version upgrade # Returns 0 on success, 1 on failure ubuntu_do_upgrade() # Prepare system before upgrade (apt update, dist-upgrade) ubuntu_prepare_upgrade() # Setup resume mechanism for after reboot ubuntu_setup_resume() # Cleanup resume mechanism after completion ubuntu_cleanup_resume() ``` ## Dependencies - Sources: logging.sh, os_detect.sh - External: do-release-upgrade, apt, lsb_release ## Design Decisions ### Why query do-release-upgrade instead of hardcoding? Hardcoding version numbers (like "25.10") becomes stale. By querying `do-release-upgrade -c` (check mode), we always know the actual next available upgrade. This future-proofs the code. ### Why check disk space? do-release-upgrade downloads hundreds of MB of packages and needs space for temporary files. Running out of disk mid-upgrade is catastrophic. We require 5GB free (conservative estimate). ### Why not use unattended-upgrade? unattended-upgrade is for security patches within a version, not for full distribution upgrades. do-release-upgrade is the official tool for version upgrades. ## Testing Notes - Test on 22.04, 24.04, 24.10 base images - Verify upgrade path calculation is correct - Ensure preflight checks catch common issues","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:19:52.130051-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","close_reason":"Created ubuntu_upgrade.sh with all core functions: version detection, upgrade path calculation, preflight checks, and upgrade execution. All 20 functions implemented and tested."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9y9x","title":"EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements","description":"# EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements ## Strategic Goal Make it effortless for users to discover what's installed, understand their environment, and continue learning—without requiring internet access or running heavy verification checks. ## Background & Rationale ### The Problem After ACFS installation, users face a discoverability gap: 1. **"What do I have?"** - No quick way to see installed tools, their aliases, or current state 2. **"What can I do?"** - 50+ aliases hidden in zshrc, not easily discoverable 3. **"How do I continue learning?"** - Onboard progress is basic, lessons are passive 4. **"I'm offline, now what?"** - Web wizard requires internet; no local reference ### Why This Matters - Beginners feel overwhelmed by unfamiliar environment - Power users forget aliases they don't use daily - SSH disconnections + reconnections lose context - The "flywheel" concept requires knowing what tools exist ### Current State Analysis - `acfs doctor`: Heavy (60+ checks), verification-focused, not quick-reference - `onboard`: Good lessons but passive, basic progress tracking - `acfs.zshrc`: Contains aliases but users don't read config files - Web wizard: Excellent but requires internet ### Proposed Solution Three complementary enhancements: 1. **`acfs info`** - Lightning-fast quick reference command - Shows: IP, hostname, uptime, installed tools, key aliases, onboard progress - Outputs: Terminal (default), JSON (--json), HTML (--html) - Design: Read state files only, NO verification/testing 2. **Enhanced Onboard Experience** - Progress visualization (percentage, progress bar) - "Try It" sections with expected output examples - Cheatsheet integration for command discovery - Completion celebrations 3. **Command Discovery System** - Categorized alias/command reference - Available via: `acfs help --commands`, `onboard --cheatsheet` - Extracted programmatically from zshrc (not duplicated) 4. **Static Dashboard Generation** (Optional Extension) - Generate HTML from `acfs info --html` - Serve on-demand with `acfs dashboard serve` - Works completely offline ## Success Criteria - [ ] User can see their full setup in <1 second with `acfs info` - [ ] User can discover all available aliases without reading zshrc - [ ] User can see onboard progress at a glance - [ ] User can access reference material offline - [ ] No duplication with existing `acfs doctor` functionality ## Non-Goals - Real-time monitoring dashboard (out of scope) - Persistent web server (security concerns) - Replacing the web wizard (it serves different purpose) ## Dependencies - None (enhances existing infrastructure) ## Downstream Impact - All feature beads in this epic depend on this for context - Implementation order matters (see dependency graph)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:51:25.132198-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T15:01:03.690637-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T15:01:03.690637-05:00","close_reason":"EPIC complete: Strategy defined, success criteria documented. Downstream features (bags, d6my, gy0i) now unblocked for implementation."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","title":"Add Tailscale auth guidance to post-install summary","description":"## Task Update the post-install summary in `install.sh` to include Tailscale authentication guidance. ## Current State The installer prints a summary at the end with next steps. We need to add Tailscale auth. ## Implementation In `print_summary()` function, add: ```bash # Check if Tailscale needs auth if command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then local ts_status ts_status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"') if [[ "$ts_status" != "Running" ]]; then echo "" echo " 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access):" echo " sudo tailscale up" echo " → Log in with your Google account" echo " → Then access this VPS from anywhere!" echo "" else local ts_ip ts_ip=$(tailscale ip -4 2>/dev/null) echo "" echo " ✓ Tailscale connected: $ts_ip" echo "" fi fi ``` ## Ordering in Summary The post-install summary should guide users through auth in a logical order: 1. **Tailscale** (first - enables remote access) 2. **Claude Code** (primary coding agent) 3. **Codex CLI** (secondary agent) 4. **Gemini CLI** (uses native Google, likely already authenticated) 5. **GitHub** (if using git) ## Example Output ``` ============================================================ ACFS Installation Complete! ============================================================ Next Steps - Authenticate Your Services: 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access): sudo tailscale up → Log in with your Google account → Then access this VPS from anywhere! 🤖 Claude Code: claude → Log in with your Anthropic account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Codex CLI: codex login → Log in with your ChatGPT account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Gemini CLI: gemini → Log in with your Google account Pro tip: Use the same Google account for all services! ============================================================ ``` ## Considerations - Only show auth steps for services that need it - Show ✓ for services already authenticated - Keep it concise but actionable","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:34.656476-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","close_reason":"Tailscale auth guidance added to print_summary() in commit 4ee3080","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bt5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:15.487935-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a24x","title":"Fix brittle YAML parsing in security.sh","description":"","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-23T14:37:03.650348-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-23T14:37:03.650348-05:00"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a24x","title":"Fix brittle YAML parsing in security.sh","description":"","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-23T14:37:03.650348-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-23T14:54:45.238996-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-23T14:54:45.238996-05:00","close_reason":"Hardened checksums.yaml parsing in scripts/lib/security.sh (scoped installers section, clears stale entries, supports uppercase hex)."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","title":"Add 'Why VPS?' conceptual section before rent-vps","description":"# Task: Add 'Why VPS?' Conceptual Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4) ## Description Before asking users to rent a VPS, explain WHY they need one. Currently we jump straight to "pick a provider" without context. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx Add a section at the top (or consider a new interstitial step): \`\`\`tsx A VPS (Virtual Private Server) is like having a computer that runs 24/7 in a datacenter. Here's why it matters for AI-assisted coding:
  • Always on: Your AI agents keep working even when your laptop is closed. Start a task, close your laptop, come back to results.
  • Persistent sessions: Your work continues exactly where you left off, even if your home internet drops.
  • Work from anywhere: Connect from your laptop, phone, or any computer. Same environment everywhere.
  • Separation: Keep AI coding separate from your personal computer. No battery drain, no cluttering your system.

Think of it as your "engine room" — the real work happens there, and your laptop is just the remote control.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Appears before VPS provider selection - [ ] Explains benefits in user terms (not technical specs) - [ ] Uses the "engine room" metaphor from onboarding - [ ] Addresses "can't I just run this on my laptop?" implicitly - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - This should feel informative, not preachy - Benefits should be concrete and relatable - Keep it scannable with bullet points - Consider making it collapsible if users already understand VPS","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:40:51.789633-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: rent-vps/page.tsx has extensive GuideExplain term='VPS' explaining why VPS is needed"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aa1","title":"Implement atomic state file writes","description":"# Task: Implement atomic state file writes ## Context Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu) ## Problem If the system crashes or SSH disconnects DURING a state write, the state file could be corrupted (partial JSON). This would cause the installer to fail on resume. ## Solution Use atomic write pattern: write to temp file, then mv (which is atomic on POSIX): ```bash save_state_atomic() { local state_file="$ACFS_HOME/state.json" local temp_file temp_file="$(mktemp)" # Write to temp file cat > "$temp_file" <
